questions.specification: A text editor needs to implement an undo functionality using stacks.

parts.content: Describe how a stack can be used to implement the undo feature.
parts.markscheme:
**Describe** how each user action is pushed onto the stack when performed. <marks>A1</marks>

**Explain** how the undo operation pops the last action from the stack. <marks>A1</marks>

**Outline** how this reverses actions in the opposite order they were performed (LIFO). <marks>R1</marks>

[3 marks]

steps.title: Pushing Actions onto Stack
steps.content:
In a text editor, every discrete user action—such as typing a word, deleting a sentence, or formatting text—is treated as a data object. Whenever the user performs one of these actions, the system performs a **push** operation, adding that action to the top of the 'undo' stack.

This ensures that the history of what the user has done is stored sequentially.

highlights.text: Describe how each user action is pushed onto the stack when performed.

steps.title: Popping for Undo Command
steps.content:
When the user triggers the 'undo' command (often by pressing $Ctrl+Z$), the system performs a **pop** operation. This operation removes the element currently at the top of the stack. 

The system then uses the information from this popped object to reverse the action (e.g., if the popped action was 'delete word,' the system will re-insert that word).

highlights.text: Explain how the undo operation pops the last action from the stack.

steps.title: The LIFO Logic
steps.content:
The reason a stack is perfect for this is the **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** property. Since the last action performed was the most recent one pushed onto the stack, it is naturally the first one to be popped off.

This ensures that the editor reverses actions in the exact **opposite order** to how they were originally performed, which is the expected behavior for an undo feature.

highlights.text: Outline how this reverses actions in the opposite order they were performed (LIFO).

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To get full marks on this IB question, ensure you mention these three components:
1. **Pushing** user actions as they occur ($1$ mark).
2. **Popping** the top action when undo is requested ($1$ mark).
3. Explaining how **LIFO** ensures the reverse chronological order ($1$ mark).

This concept is a core part of **Topic 5: Abstract Data Structures**. If you're finding stacks interesting, you might also want to look at **Topic 5.1.5: Queues**, which use a different logic called $FIFO$.

parts.content: Construct code to push actions onto the stack when changes are made.

$$\text{Stack}<\text{Action}> \text{ undoStack} = \text{new Stack}<>();$$

This represents the step of capturing the action as an object with its required parameters.

This follows the logic of Abstract Data Types (ADTs) where the Stack follows a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) principle.

steps.title: Identify the stack's role
steps.content: To implement an undo feature, we use a **Stack** because it follows the **Last-In, First-Out (LIFO)** principle. The last action performed is the first one we want to "undo." This is covered in **Topic 5: Abstract data structures**, specifically section **5.1.4 Stacks**.

steps.title: Initialize the stack structure
steps.content:
First, we must create or declare the stack that will hold our action objects. In IB pseudocode or Java-style syntax, we define a collection that supports stack operations.

$$UNDO\_STACK = new\ Stack()$$

highlights.text: create or access the stack data structure

steps.title: Capture the action object
steps.content:
Every time a change is made in the text editor (like typing a character or deleting a word), we need to encapsulate that event into an object. Let's assume we have a class called `Action` that stores the details of what happened.

$$Action\ currentAction = new\ Action("type", "Hello")$$

This captures the command and its parameters so it can be replayed or reversed later.

highlights.text: capture the action as an object or command with required parameters

steps.title: Perform the push operation
steps.content:
Finally, we use the `.push()` method to add the action to the top of the stack. This ensures the most recent change is always at the top.

$$\begin{aligned} &UNDO\_STACK.push(currentAction) \end{aligned}$$

By combining these steps into a method, we satisfy all three marking criteria: creating the structure, capturing the data, and performing the stack operation.

steps.title: Final code construction

parts.content: Construct code to pop actions from the stack to undo the last change.

steps.title: Understand the Stack Logic
steps.content:
To implement an "undo" feature, we use a **Stack** data structure. Stacks follow the **Last-In, First-Out (LIFO)** principle, which is perfect for undoing because the very last action performed is the first one we want to revert. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic 5: Abstract data structures**. Before we can remove an item, we must always ensure the stack isn't empty to avoid errors.

highlights.text: undo functionality using stacks

steps.title: Check for Empty Stack
steps.content:
In Java, we use the `.isEmpty()` method to check if the stack contains any elements. To award the first mark, we must construct an `if` statement that checks if the stack is **not** empty using the logical NOT operator (`!`).

$$\text{if (!undoStack.isEmpty())}$$

highlights.text: check if the stack is not empty using !undoStack.isEmpty()

steps.title: Pop and Execute Action
steps.content:
Once we've confirmed the stack has data, we use the `.pop()` method. This method does two things: it retrieves the top element and removes it from the stack. 

After popping the action, we call the `undo()` method on that specific object to revert the change in the text editor. This earns the second mark.

highlights.text: pop actions from the stack

steps.title: Final Java Implementation
Check stack status
steps.content:
Before we can perform an undo operation, we must ensure there is actually something to undo! Attempting to remove an item from an empty stack causes an error. In IB pseudocode, we use a conditional check to see if the stack is **not** empty.

This uses concepts from **Topic 5.1.4: Stacks**, where we learn that managing the "underflow" (trying to pop from an empty structure) is a critical part of algorithm design.

steps.title: Remove the last action
steps.content:
Once we know the stack contains data, we use the `pop()` operation. Because a stack is a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** structure, `pop()` will automatically give us the very last action the user performed.

We store this result in a variable so we can interact with it in the next step.

steps.title: Execute the undo logic
steps.content: After popping the action object from the stack, we need to call its internal logic to actually reverse the change in the text editor. We do this by calling a method (like `.undo()`) on the object we just retrieved.

steps.title: Construct the pseudocode
steps.content:
Combining these steps into the standard IB notation, we use the `if` and `NOT` keywords. Note that in pseudocode, we often use `isEmpty()` or `isEmpty()` as a standard method for collections.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text{if NOT undoStack.isEmpty() then} \\ & \quad \text{ACTION = undoStack.pop()} \\ & \quad \text{ACTION.undo()} \\ & \text{end if} \end{aligned}$$

questions.specification: A compiler uses a stack to evaluate expressions in reverse Polish notation (RPN).

parts.content: Outline the features of the abstract data type (ADT) stack.
parts.markscheme:
- Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) ordering <marks>1</marks>
- Primary operations are `push` (add to top) and `pop` (remove from top) <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define the LIFO principle
steps.content:
A stack is a linear data structure that follows the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle. This means that the last element added to the stack is the very first one to be removed. 

Think of it like a physical stack of cafeteria trays: you place a new tray on the top, and when someone needs a tray, they take the one from the top (the one most recently placed there).

highlights.text: Outline the features of the abstract data type (ADT) stack.

steps.title: Identify primary operations
steps.content:
The ADT stack is defined by two fundamental operations that occur at only one end, called the "top":

1.  **`push`**: This operation adds a new item to the top of the stack.
2.  **`pop`**: This operation removes the item that is currently at the top of the stack.

In the context of Reverse Polish Notation (RPN), the compiler uses these operations to hold operands until an operator is encountered.

steps.title: Review Markscheme Requirements
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks for this question, you must mention both the ordering logic and the specific names of the operations:

- **Mark 1**: State that it is a **LIFO** (Last-In-First-Out) structure.
- **Mark 2**: Identify the primary operations as **`push`** and **`pop`**.

This fundamental understanding is covered in **Topic 5.1.4: Stacks** of the IB Computer Science syllabus.

steps.title: The structural access point
steps.content: A stack is an abstract data type defined by its physical structure: it has only one accessible end, known as the **top**. Unlike an array where you can access any index, a stack restricts all additions and removals to this single point.

steps.title: Define LIFO logic
steps.content: Because items can only be added or removed from the top, the stack follows a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** ordering. This means the most recent element placed onto the stack is the very first one to be taken off.

highlights.text: Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) ordering

steps.title: Identify primary operations
steps.content:
To interact with this structure, we use two primary operations:

- `push`: Adds a new element to the top of the stack.
- `pop`: Removes the element currently at the top of the stack.

In the context of Reverse Polish Notation (RPN), the compiler 'pushes' operands onto the stack and 'pops' them when it encounters an operator.

highlights.text: Primary operations are push (add to top) and pop (remove from top)

parts.content: Explain why a stack is suitable for expression evaluation.
parts.markscheme:
- Stacks allow easy access to the most recently added elements (operands) <marks>1</marks>
- Suitable for tracking operands and operators during expression evaluation where operands are stored until an operator is encountered <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define the LIFO property
steps.content: A stack is a linear data structure that operates on the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle. This means that the most recently added item is always the first one to be removed. In the context of expression evaluation, this property is crucial because it allows immediate access to the values needed for an operation.

steps.title: Store operands until needed
steps.content: In Reverse Polish Notation (RPN), operands (numbers) appear before their operators. As the compiler reads the expression from left to right, it uses the stack to store these operands. They are held there until an operator is encountered, at which point the calculation can begin.

highlights.text: operands are stored until an operator is encountered

steps.title: Access recent operands immediately
steps.content: When an operator (like $+$ or $\times$) is reached, it needs to be applied to the two most recently seen operands. Because of the **LIFO property**, these two operands are conveniently located at the very top of the stack. The stack provides easy and direct access to these most recently added elements, allowing the compiler to 'pop' them, perform the math, and 'push' the result back for the next step.

highlights.text: Stacks allow easy access to the most recently added elements (operands)

steps.title: Summary of suitability
steps.content: To summarize, a stack is suitable because its LIFO nature perfectly matches the requirements of RPN evaluation: it efficiently tracks operands and ensures that the specific values required for an operator are always the first ones retrieved.

steps.title: The Stack as a Buffer
steps.content:
In Reverse Polish Notation (RPN), operands (numbers) always precede their operators. Because of this, we need a way to store these numbers temporarily while we wait for an operator to appear. The stack serves as an **algorithmic storage buffer**, holding operands in the exact order they are received.

This concept is explored in **Topic 5.1: Abstract data structures**, specifically focusing on how stacks manage data.

steps.title: Accessing the Recent Elements
steps.content:
The defining characteristic of a stack is its **Last-In, First-Out (LIFO)** nature. When evaluating an expression, the operator always applies to the most recently seen numbers. 

A stack is suitable because it allows **easy access to the most recently added elements (operands)** by simply 'popping' them from the top when an operator is encountered.

highlights.text: Stacks allow easy access to the most recently added elements (operands)

steps.title: Tracking and Evaluation
steps.content:
The stack tracks the state of the calculation. It is **suitable for tracking operands and operators** because it keeps the operands in a waiting state. 

Once an operator (like $+$ or $\times$) is encountered, the stack provides the necessary operands, the calculation is performed, and the result is pushed back onto the stack to be used for the next part of the expression. This removes the need for complex parentheses.

highlights.text: Suitable for tracking operands and operators during expression evaluation where operands are stored until an operator is encountered

steps.title: Final Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks for this question, your explanation should focus on these two points:
1. The LIFO nature provides quick access to the most recently stored operands.
2. The stack effectively manages the 'storage' phase (keeping operands) and the 'execution' phase (when an operator appears).

parts.content: Construct methods `push` and `pop` for a static implementation of a stack to handle integer operands.

methods.label: Top Pointer at Last Occupied Index

steps.title: Define the Stack Logic
steps.content:
Before we write the code, let's understand the **Top Pointer at Last Occupied Index** approach. In this method, the variable `top` always points to the index of the most recently added item. 

Because arrays are 0-indexed, if the stack is empty, there is no "last occupied index," so we initialize `top = -1`. This is a core concept from **Topic 5.1: Abstract Data Structures**.

steps.title: Implement the Push Signature
steps.content: The `push` method is used to add an integer to the stack. It doesn't need to return anything, so we use the `void` return type. It takes one parameter: the integer `value` we want to store.

highlights.text: Construct methods `push` and `pop`
handle integer operands

steps.title: Handle Stack Overflow (Push)
steps.content:
Before adding an item, we must check if the stack is full (Stack Overflow). Since the array has a fixed `capacity`, it is full when `top` reaches the last available index, which is `capacity - 1`.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Condition: } & \text{if } (top == capacity - 1) \\ \text{Action: } & \text{Stack is full, cannot add more.} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Add Element (Push)
steps.content:
Using the **Last Occupied Index** approach, we must increment the pointer *before* adding the new value. We can do this efficiently using the pre-increment operator `++top`.

```java
stack[++top] = value;
```
This line first increases `top` and then places the `value` at that new index.

steps.title: Implement the Pop Signature
steps.content: The `pop` method removes and returns the top item from the stack. Since our stack handles integers, the return type must be `int`.

highlights.text: Construct methods `push` and `pop`

steps.title: Handle Stack Underflow (Pop)
steps.content:
We cannot remove an item if the stack is empty (Stack Underflow). Since we initialized `top` to -1, the stack is empty whenever `top` is still -1.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Condition: } & \text{if } (top == -1) \\ \text{Action: } & \text{Stack is empty, return error value (e.g., -1).} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Remove Element (Pop)
steps.content:
To pop the item, we return the value currently at `top` and then decrement the pointer. This is done using the post-decrement operator `top--`.

```java
return stack[top--];
```
This returns the value at the current index and *afterward* reduces `top` by 1 so it points to the new "last occupied" index.

methods.label: Top Pointer at Next Available Index

steps.title: Define the Stack Pointers
steps.content:
In the **Top Pointer at Next Available Index** approach, we initialize the pointer `top` to $0$. This means `top` always points to the empty slot where the next pushed item will go. 

Before we write the methods, we assume we have a class with:
- An array `stack[]` of a fixed size.
- An integer `top` initialized to $0$.

steps.title: The Push Method: Signature
steps.content:
The `push` method needs to receive an integer as a parameter and does not need to return a value. The standard IB method signature is:

$$public\ void\ push(int\ value)$$

This would earn the first mark for the method signature.

highlights.text: push
integer operands

steps.title: Push: Check for Overflow
steps.content:
Since we are using a **static** implementation (a fixed-size array), we must check if the stack is full before adding a new item. 

If `top` is equal to the length of the array, there are no more empty slots left. 

$$\begin{aligned} &if\ (top == stack.length) \\ &\quad // Handle\ overflow \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: static implementation

steps.title: Push: Implementation Logic
steps.content:
Following the **Next Available Index** approach, we store the new value at the current `top` index and then increment `top` to the next empty spot.

$$\begin{aligned} &stack[top] = value; \\ &top++; \end{aligned}$$

Alternatively, this can be written concisely using the post-increment operator: `stack[top++] = value;`.

steps.title: The Pop Method: Signature
steps.content:
The `pop` method should return the integer that was most recently added and removed from the stack. The signature is:

$$public\ int\ pop()$$

This earns the mark for the correct `pop` method signature.

highlights.text: pop

steps.title: Pop: Check for Underflow
steps.content:
We cannot remove an item from an empty stack. In this approach, if `top == 0`, it means no items have been added (or all have been removed).

$$\begin{aligned} &if\ (top == 0) \\ &\quad // Handle\ underflow \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Pop: Implementation Logic
steps.content:
Because `top` points to the *next available* (empty) slot, the actual data is at `top - 1`. We must decrement the pointer first, and then retrieve the value.

$$\begin{aligned} &top--; \\ &return\ stack[top]; \end{aligned}$$

This can be written concisely using the pre-decrement operator: `return stack[--top];`.

steps.title: Final Implementation Summary
steps.content:
Here is how the final methods look using the **Next Available Index** logic required for this specific approach:

$$\begin{aligned} &public\ void\ push(int\ value) \{ \\ &\quad if\ (top == stack.length)\ return; \\ &\quad stack[top] = value; \\ &\quad top++; \\ &\} \\ \\ &public\ int\ pop() \{ \\ &\quad if\ (top == 0)\ return\ -1; \\ &\quad top--; \\ &\quad return\ stack[top]; \\ &\} \end{aligned}$$

questions.specification: A gaming application uses recursion to generate a maze.

parts.content: Describe an application of recursive algorithms in maze generation.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe how recursion divides the maze area into smaller sections by placing walls and creating gaps (e.g., the recursive division algorithm) <marks>M1</marks>

- Explain how recursion simplifies the overall process by solving sub-problems (sub-grids) until a base case of minimal size is reached <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Understand the Recursive Division Method
steps.content: In maze generation, the **Recursive Division** algorithm works by taking a large, empty space and "dividing" it into smaller and smaller pieces. This is a classic example of a "divide and conquer" strategy, which is a core concept in recursive thinking (covered in **Topic 5.1.1: Recognizing Recursive Thinking**).

steps.title: The Division Process
steps.content:
The algorithm starts with an empty rectangular grid. It places a wall (either horizontally or vertically) to split the area into two smaller rectangular sub-grids. 

To ensure the maze is solvable, the algorithm must create a **gap** (a doorway) at a random location along that wall. This ensures that a path exists between the two newly created sections.

highlights.text: Describe how recursion divides the maze area into smaller sections by placing walls and creating gaps (e.g., the recursive division algorithm)

steps.title: Applying Recursion to Sub-problems
steps.content: The beauty of recursion here is that the algorithm then treats each of the two smaller sub-grids as a "new" maze-generation problem. It calls itself (the recursive step) to divide those sub-sections again. This simplifies the complex task of building a whole maze into the repetitive task of dividing smaller and smaller rectangles.

steps.title: Defining the Base Case
steps.content: Every recursive algorithm needs a **base case** to prevent it from running forever. In this application, the recursion stops when a sub-grid reaches a **minimal size** (for example, a $1 \times 1$ area or a rectangle too narrow to be split further). Once all sub-grids reach this base case, the complete maze is finished.

highlights.text: Explain how recursion simplifies the overall process by solving sub-problems (sub-grids) until a base case of minimal size is reached

steps.title: Define the Recursive Step
steps.content: In the **Recursive Backtracking (DFS)** approach, the algorithm begins at a specific starting cell and marks it as visited. It then randomly selects an unvisited neighbor, removes the wall between them, and performs a recursive call on that neighbor. This process treats each movement as a **sub-problem**, where the algorithm focuses on exploring from the new current cell as if it were the start of a new, smaller maze.

highlights.text: recursion to generate a maze

steps.title: Identify the Base Case
steps.content: The **base case** occurs when the algorithm reaches a cell that has no unvisited neighbors (a 'dead end'). At this point, the current recursive instance finishes, and the algorithm **backtracks** to the previous cell in the call stack to check for other available paths. This ensures the complex task of maze generation is simplified into manageable steps until the entire grid has been visited.

steps.title: Review Markscheme Alignment
steps.content:
To earn full marks, your answer must address two key points:
1.  **Sub-problems**: Mention how recursion simplifies the task by breaking the maze exploration into sub-grids or individual cell paths.
2.  **Base Case**: Explain that the recursion continues until a base case (no unvisited neighbors) is reached, leading to backtracking.

This follows the concepts found in **Topic 5.1: Abstract Data Structures**, specifically regarding recursive thinking and tracing.

parts.content: Construct a recursive method $\texttt{generateMaze}$ that divides a grid into sub-grids.

steps.title: Define the method signature
steps.content:
To start, we need to define the method header using standard Java syntax. The method should be $\texttt{public}$, return $\texttt{void}$ (as it modifies the grid object directly), and take a $\texttt{Grid}$ object as a parameter.

$$\texttt{public void generateMaze(Grid grid)}$$

highlights.text: appropriate method signature

steps.title: Establish the base case
steps.content:
Every recursive function requires a base case to prevent infinite recursion and a $\texttt{StackOverflowError}$. In this maze generation algorithm, the recursion should stop when the current sub-grid becomes too small to be divided further (e.g., its width or height is less than a constant $MIN\_SIZE$).

$$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{if (grid.getHeight() < MIN\_SIZE || grid.getWidth() < MIN\_SIZE) {} \\ &\quad \texttt{return;} \\ &\texttt{}} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: base case check to prevent infinite recursion

steps.title: Add walls and passages
steps.content:
The core logic of the algorithm involves dividing the current grid. We do this by adding a wall and then creating at least one passage through that wall to ensure the maze remains solvable.

$$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{Wall wall = grid.addRandomWall();} \\ &\texttt{wall.addPassage();} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: logic of adding walls to divide the grid and creating passages

steps.title: Implement recursive calls
steps.content:
Finally, we call the $\texttt{generateMaze}$ method recursively on the new sub-grids created by the wall. This breaks the problem down into smaller and smaller pieces until the base case is met.

$$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(1));} \\ &\texttt{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(2));} \\ &\texttt{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(3));} \\ &\texttt{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(4));} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: correctly structured recursive calls

steps.title: Consolidate the final code
steps.content:
Combining these steps gives us a complete recursive method. Notice how the structure mirrors the "Divide and Conquer" strategy often used in **Topic 4: Computational Thinking**.

$$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{public void generateMaze(Grid grid) {} \\ &\quad \texttt{if (grid.getHeight() < MIN\_SIZE || grid.getWidth() < MIN\_SIZE) {} \\ &\quad \quad \texttt{return;} \\ &\quad \texttt{}} \\ \\ &\quad \texttt{Wall wall = grid.addRandomWall();} \\ &\quad \texttt{wall.addPassage();} \\ \\ &\quad \texttt{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(1));} \\ &\quad \texttt{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(2));} \\ &\quad \texttt{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(3));} \\ &\quad \texttt{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(4));} \\ &\texttt{}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Define the method signature
steps.content:
To start, we need to define the method header. In IB pseudocode, we use the keyword `method` followed by the name and the parameters. Since the method performs an action on a grid, it should accept a `grid` object as a parameter.

$$\text{method generateMaze(grid)}$$

highlights.text: appropriate method signature

steps.title: Establish the base case
steps.content:
Recursion must eventually stop to prevent an infinite loop (which would cause a stack overflow). We check if the grid is too small to be divided further. If it is, we use the `return` statement to exit the current call.

$$\begin{aligned} &\textbf{if } \text{grid.getHeight() < MIN_SIZE } \textbf{OR } \text{grid.getWidth() < MIN_SIZE } \textbf{then} \\ &\quad \textbf{return} \\ &\textbf{end if} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: base case check to prevent infinite recursion

steps.title: Implement the division logic
steps.content:
Once we know the grid is large enough, we perform the "maze-making" logic. This involves adding a wall to split the space and ensuring a passage is created so the maze remains solvable.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{wall = grid.addRandomWall()} \\ &\text{wall.addPassage()} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: logic of adding walls to divide the grid and creating passages

steps.title: Execute recursive calls
steps.content:
Finally, the method must call itself on the smaller areas (sub-grids) created by the division. This repeat process continues until all sub-grids reach the minimum size defined in our base case.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(1))} \\ &\text{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(2))} \\ &\text{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(3))} \\ &\text{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(4))} \\ &\textbf{end method} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: recursive calls on each of the resulting sub-grids

steps.title: Final pseudocode structure
steps.content:
Here is the complete recursive algorithm constructed using IB pseudocode standards:

$$\begin{aligned} &\textbf{method } \text{generateMaze(grid)} \\ &\quad \textbf{if } \text{grid.getHeight() < MIN_SIZE } \textbf{then} \\ &\quad \quad \textbf{return} \\ &\quad \textbf{end if} \\ &\\ &\quad \text{wall = grid.addRandomWall()} \\ &\quad \text{wall.addPassage()} \\ &\\ &\quad \text{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(1))} \\ &\quad \text{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(2))} \\ &\quad \text{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(3))} \\ &\quad \text{generateMaze(grid.getSubgrid(4))} \\ &\textbf{end method} \end{aligned}$$

This approach uses the concepts found in **Topic 5.1: Abstract data structures** regarding recursive thinking and tracing.

parts.content: Explain why recursive algorithms are rarely used in practice despite being elegant.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that recursive algorithms incur significant memory overhead due to multiple function calls being stored on the call stack <marks>R1</marks>

- Identify the risk of a stack overflow error if the recursion depth becomes too large (e.g., in a very large maze) <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the call stack
steps.content:
To understand why recursion is memory-intensive, we have to look at how the computer handles function calls. Every time a recursive function calls itself, the operating system allocates a block of memory called a **stack frame** on the **call stack**. 

In our maze example, each new path the algorithm explores adds a new frame to the stack to keep track of local variables, parameters, and the return address. If the maze is complex, these frames pile up quickly.

steps.title: Identify memory overhead
steps.content:
The first reason recursive algorithms are often avoided is **significant memory overhead**. Unlike an iterative loop (like a `for` or `while` loop) which uses a fixed amount of memory, recursion requires more RAM for every single level of depth. 

This means that for a large maze, the program might consume a disproportionate amount of system resources just to keep track of its own progress.

steps.title: Evaluate stack overflow risk
steps.content:
The most critical technical risk is a **stack overflow error**. Computers allocate a finite, limited amount of memory to the call stack. 

If the recursion depth (the number of active function calls) becomes too large—which is very likely with a large or complex maze—the stack will run out of space. This causes the application to crash immediately because it can no longer store the information needed to continue or return from the functions.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks, your explanation should connect the **resource usage** (memory/stack frames) to the **system failure** (stack overflow).

- **Mark 1:** Recursive algorithms incur high memory overhead because multiple function calls are stored on the call stack simultaneously.
- **Mark 2:** This creates a risk of a stack overflow error if the recursion depth exceeds the stack's memory limit (e.g., in a very large maze).

parts.content: In the context of modular programming, explain two advantages of using local variables rather than global variables.
parts.markscheme:
- **Reduced side effects / Data Integrity**: Local variables are only accessible within their specific sub-program, preventing accidental modification by other parts of the program and making the code more reliable <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Memory Efficiency**: Local variables are typically created when the sub-program is called and destroyed when it finishes, freeing up memory; whereas global variables persist for the entire program duration <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content:
An array named `SALES` contains 50 numerical values representing daily transaction amounts. 

Construct a pseudocode algorithm that calculates and outputs the average of all transactions that are strictly greater than $100.00$. If no transactions meet this criterion, the algorithm should output the message "No qualifying sales".
parts.markscheme:
- Correct initialization of `TOTAL` and `COUNT` variables <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct loop structure to iterate through all 50 elements (e.g., `FOR I FROM 0 TO 49`) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct `IF` condition to check if `SALES[I] > 100` <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct accumulation of `TOTAL` and incrementing of `COUNT` inside the loop <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct check for `COUNT > 0` after the loop to prevent division by zero <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct output of the calculated average or the specific error message <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Example Answer:**
```
TOTAL = 0
COUNT = 0
FOR I FROM 0 TO 49
    IF SALES[I] > 100 THEN
        TOTAL = TOTAL + SALES[I]
        COUNT = COUNT + 1
    END IF
END FOR

IF COUNT > 0 THEN
    AVERAGE = TOTAL / COUNT
    OUTPUT AVERAGE
ELSE
    OUTPUT "No qualifying sales"
END IF
```

questions.specification: A web browser uses a linked list to manage the history of visited pages.

parts.content: Construct a class `HistoryNode` with a `String` `url` and an object reference to the next node.

steps.title: Identify requirements
steps.content:
To build the `HistoryNode` class, we need to follow the **Standard Class Implementation**. This involves three main parts:
1. Declaring the class.
2. Defining private attributes for data and the next reference.
3. Creating a constructor to initialize these values.

This follows the principles of **encapsulation** covered in **Topic D: Object-oriented programming**.

highlights.text: Construct a class HistoryNode

steps.title: Declare the class
steps.content:
First, we declare the class using the name specified in the question. In Java, we use the `public` modifier so it can be accessed by the rest of the program.

$$ \text{public class HistoryNode \{ } $$

This earns the first mark for the correct class header.

highlights.text: public class HistoryNode

steps.title: Declare private attributes
steps.content:
Next, we add the attributes. To ensure encapsulation, we mark them as `private`. We need one `String` for the URL and one reference of type `HistoryNode` to point to the next node in the list.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{private String url;} \\ &\text{private HistoryNode next;} \end{aligned}$$

This fulfills the requirements for the second and third marks.

highlights.text: String url
object reference to the next node

steps.title: Construct the constructor
steps.content:
Finally, we implement the constructor. It must accept a `String` parameter to initialize the `url`. As per the **Standard Class Implementation**, we must also explicitly set the `next` reference to `null` to indicate that a new node doesn't point to anything yet.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{public HistoryNode(String url) \{} \\ &\quad \text{this.url = url;} \\ &\quad \text{this.next = null;} \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$

This completes the class and secures the final mark.

highlights.text: initializes the url and sets next to null

steps.title: Final Implementation
steps.content:
Combining everything together, your complete code should look like this:

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{public class HistoryNode \{} \\ &\quad \text{private String url;} \\ &\quad \text{private HistoryNode next;} \\ \\ &\quad \text{public HistoryNode(String url) \{} \\ &\quad \quad \text{this.url = url;} \\ &\quad \quad \text{this.next = null;} \\ &\quad \text{\}} \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Construct a method `addPage` to add a new page to the end of the history list. Assume that `head` is a reference to the first node in the list.

steps.title: Define the method signature
steps.content:
To start, we need to define the method as specified. The question asks for a method named `addPage` that takes a $String$ parameter representing the URL. Since this method modifies the list and doesn't return a value, we use the $void$ return type.

$$public\ void\ addPage(String\ url)$$

This is the standard way to define a behavior for a linked list in Topic 5: Abstract Data Structures.

highlights.text: Construct a method addPage

steps.title: Create the new node
steps.content:
Before we can add anything to the list, we must encapsulate the $url$ data into a new node object. We instantiate a new `HistoryNode` and pass the $url$ to its constructor.

$$HistoryNode\ newNode = new\ HistoryNode(url);$$

steps.title: Check for empty list
steps.content:
We must handle the edge case where the list is currently empty. In a linked list, if the list is empty, the $head$ reference will be $null$. If this condition is true, our new node simply becomes the $head$ of the list.

$$\begin{aligned} &if\ (head == null) \{ \\ &\quad head = newNode; \\ &\} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: head is a reference to the first node

steps.title: Traverse to the end
steps.content:
If the list is not empty, we need to find the current last node to attach our $newNode$ to it. We use a temporary pointer, often called $current$, and initialize it at the $head$. We then use a $while$ loop to move through the list as long as there is a "next" node available.

$$\begin{aligned} &HistoryNode\ current = head; \\ &while\ (current.getNext() != null) \{ \\ &\quad current = current.getNext(); \\ &\} \end{aligned}$$

The loop stops when $current$ points to the node where $getNext()$ is $null$, which is the definition of the last node.

highlights.text: add a new page to the end

steps.title: Link the new node
steps.content:
Now that $current$ is referencing the last node in the history, we simply update its "next" pointer to point to our newly created node. This effectively appends the page to the end of the history.

$$current.setNext(newNode);$$

steps.title: Final implementation
Define the method signature
steps.content:
To start, we define the method signature as required by the question. The method should be `public`, return nothing (`void`), and take a `String` parameter representing the URL of the new page.

$$ \text{public void addPage(String url)} $$

highlights.text: public void addPage(String url)

steps.title: Handle the empty list
steps.content:
Before we can traverse the list, we must check if the list is empty. If the `head` reference is `null`, it means there are no pages in the history yet. In this case, we simply point `head` to our new node and stop.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{HistoryNode newNode = new HistoryNode(url);} \\ &\text{if (head == null) \{} \\ &\quad \text{head = newNode;} \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: if (head == null)

steps.title: Initiate recursive traversal
steps.content:
If the list is not empty, we need to find the very last node to attach our new page. Using the **Recursive Traversal** approach, we call a recursive helper method (let's call it `findEndAndAdd`) starting from the `head` node.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{else \{} \\ &\quad \text{findEndAndAdd(head, newNode);} \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$ 

This approach is covered in **Topic 5.1: Abstract data structures**, specifically regarding recursive thinking and linked list operations.

steps.title: Identify the base case
steps.content:
Inside our recursive helper method, we need a **base case**. The base case occurs when we reach the last node in the list. We know a node is the last one if its `next` pointer is `null`. 

When we find this node, we perform the link by setting its `next` reference to our new node.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{if (current.getNext() == null) \{} \\ &\quad \text{current.setNext(newNode);} \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: Set the next reference of the last node

steps.title: Apply the recursive step
steps.content:
If the current node is not the last node (i.e., `getNext()` is not `null`), we move to the next node by calling the helper method again. This is the **recursive step**, which moves our pointer further down the list.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{else \{} \\ &\quad \text{findEndAndAdd(current.getNext(), newNode);} \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final recursive implementation
steps.content:
Combining these steps, here is the full implementation using the recursive traversal method. This earns the full 4 marks by handling the signature, the empty list condition, traversal, and the final link.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{public void addPage(String url) \{} \\ &\quad \text{HistoryNode newNode = new HistoryNode(url);} \\ &\quad \text{if (head == null) head = newNode;} \\ &\quad \text{else findEndAndAdd(head, newNode);} \\ &\text{\}} \\ \\ &\text{private void findEndAndAdd(HistoryNode current, HistoryNode newNode) \{} \\ &\quad \text{if (current.getNext() == null) current.setNext(newNode);} \\ &\quad \text{else findEndAndAdd(current.getNext(), newNode);} \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Explain one advantage of using object references in implementing the history list.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that object references allow for dynamic memory allocation and pointers <marks>R1</marks>

- **Explain** that this allows the list structure to grow and shrink at runtime as needed without requiring a fixed initial size <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the core concept
steps.content:
To answer this question, we need to look at how **object references** handle memory compared to static structures like arrays. This is covered in **Topic 5.1.9: Static and Dynamic Data Structures**. 

In a linked list, each "node" (a visited page) is an object, and we use references (pointers) to connect them. This method relies on **dynamic memory allocation**.

steps.title: State the mechanism
steps.content:
The first mark is awarded for stating that object references enable **dynamic memory allocation**. 

Instead of the computer reserving a single, continuous block of memory when the browser starts, object references allow the program to request small pieces of memory for each new node only when they are actually needed.

highlights.text: object references allow for dynamic memory allocation and pointers

steps.title: Explain the runtime benefit
steps.content:
The second mark is for explaining the practical advantage: the list can **grow and shrink at runtime**.

Because the browser doesn't have to define a **fixed initial size**, it won't waste memory by over-allocating space for 1,000 pages if you only visit 5. Conversely, the history won't "run out of room" like a fixed-size array would, as it can keep adding new object references as long as there is free system memory.

highlights.text: allows the list structure to grow and shrink at runtime as needed without requiring a fixed initial size

steps.title: Define object references
steps.content: In a linked list, each "page" in the browser history is an object. **Object references** act as pointers that store the memory address of the next node. Unlike a static array where elements must be stored in one continuous block of memory, references allow nodes to be scattered anywhere in the system's memory.

steps.title: Explain dynamic memory usage
steps.content:
Because we use references, the history list uses **dynamic memory allocation**. This means the computer only allocates memory for a new page when the user actually visits it. 

This is a huge advantage because we don't have to decide on a "maximum size" for the history list when the browser starts. If you're interested in why this matters, you can review **Topic 5.1.9: Static and Dynamic Data Structures**.

highlights.text: object references allow for dynamic memory allocation and pointers

steps.title: Efficiency in manipulation
steps.content:
Using the **Efficient Node Manipulation** approach, we see that adding or deleting a page is very fast. 

In a static array, if you wanted to insert a page at the start, you would have to shift every other element over by one spot in memory. With object references, you simply:
1. Create a new node.
2. Update its reference pointer to point to the current "head" of the list.

This allows the structure to **grow and shrink at runtime** as needed without the overhead of moving data around.

highlights.text: allows the list structure to grow and shrink at runtime as needed without requiring a fixed initial size

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To get full marks on this 2-mark question, you must connect the technical feature to the functional benefit:

*   **Mark 1:** State that object references enable dynamic memory allocation/pointers.
*   **Mark 2:** Explain that this allows the list to change size (grow/shrink) while the program is running, avoiding the limitations of a fixed-size array.

steps.title: Identify the advantage
steps.content: One significant advantage of using object references is that they allow for **dynamic memory allocation**. In a linked list, each 'node' (representing a web page) is created only when it is needed, rather than pre-allocating a fixed amount of space at the start.

highlights.text: object references

steps.title: Explain non-contiguous storage
steps.content: Because the nodes are connected by references (pointers), they can be stored in **non-contiguous memory locations**. This means the computer doesn't need to find one large, empty block of memory to fit the entire history list. Instead, it can use small, scattered 'fragments' of available memory space to store individual nodes.

steps.title: Connect to runtime flexibility
steps.content: This approach allows the history list to **grow and shrink at runtime** as the user navigates the web. Unlike a static array which has a fixed size, the linked list can keep expanding as long as there is any free memory available, regardless of where that memory is located.

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks on the exam, ensure you mention:
1.  **The Mechanism**: Object references allow for dynamic memory allocation and pointers.
2.  **The Benefit**: This enables the list to grow or shrink during execution without requiring a fixed initial size.

questions.specification: An application needs to reverse a string using recursion.

parts.content: Construct a recursive method `reverseString` that takes a `String` and returns its reverse.
Trace the execution of `reverseString("abc")`, showing all recursive calls and returns.
parts.markscheme:
- `reverseString("abc")` calls `reverseString("bc") + 'a'` <marks>1</marks>
- `reverseString("bc")` calls `reverseString("c") + 'b'` <marks>1</marks>
- `reverseString("c")` returns `"c"` (base case) <marks>1</marks>
- `reverseString("bc")` returns `"c" + 'b' = "cb"` <marks>1</marks>
- `reverseString("abc")` returns `"cb" + 'a' = "cba"` 

<note>Award maximum 4 marks for a complete and logical trace.</note>

steps.title: Understand Sequential Substitution
steps.content:
To trace this algorithm using **Sequential Substitution**, we will first expand each recursive call until we reach the simplest version (the base case). Then, we will substitute those values back into the expressions to find the final result. 

This process is a key part of **Topic 5.1.2: Tracing Recursive Algorithms** in the IB Computer Science syllabus.

steps.title: Expand the first call
steps.content:
We begin with the initial call. To reverse "abc", the function takes the substring from the second character onwards ("bc") and appends the first character ("a") to the end of that result.

$$reverseString("abc") = reverseString("bc") + "a"$$

highlights.text: reverseString("abc") calls reverseString("bc") + 'a'

steps.title: Expand the second call
steps.content:
Now we need to evaluate the call produced in the previous step. Following the same logic, we take the substring "c" and append "b" to the end.

$$reverseString("bc") = reverseString("c") + "b"$$

highlights.text: reverseString("bc") calls reverseString("c") + 'b'

steps.title: Identify the base case
steps.content:
The function continues until the string cannot be split further. When the string has only one character, it is its own reverse.

$$reverseString("c") = "c"$$

highlights.text: reverseString("c") returns "c" (base case)

steps.title: Substitute and return (Part 1)
steps.content:
Now we work backwards. Substitute the value from the base case into the expression for `reverseString("bc")`:

$$\begin{aligned} reverseString("bc") &= "c" + "b" \\ &= "cb" \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: reverseString("bc") returns "c" + 'b' = "cb"

steps.title: Substitute and return (Part 2)
steps.content:
Finally, substitute the result for `reverseString("bc")` back into our very first equation to get the final answer:

$$\begin{aligned} reverseString("abc") &= "cb" + "a" \\ &= "cba" \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: reverseString("abc") returns "cb" + 'a' = "cba"

steps.title: Identify the Recursive Structure
steps.content:
To trace `reverseString("abc")`, we need to visualize how the function breaks down the problem. In recursion, each call creates a new 'node' in our tree. Based on the markscheme, the logic follows this pattern:

$$\text{reverseString}(\text{string}) = \text{reverseString}(\text{substring starting at index 1}) + \text{first character}$$

This falls under **Topic 5.1: Abstract Data Structures**, specifically tracing recursive algorithms.

highlights.text: reverseString("abc")

steps.title: Build the Branching Tree
steps.content:
We start at the top (root) and branch down until we hit the base case. Each level represents a new function call:

$$\begin{matrix} \text{Level 0:} & \text{reverseString("abc")} \\ & \downarrow \\ \text{Level 1:} & \text{reverseString("bc")} + \text{'a'} \\ & \downarrow \\ \text{Level 2:} & \text{reverseString("c")} + \text{'b'} \end{matrix}$$

Each step down reduces the string length until only one character remains.

highlights.text: reverseString("abc") calls reverseString("bc") + 'a'

steps.title: Identify the Base Case
steps.content:
The recursion must stop somewhere so it doesn't run forever. When the function reaches a string with only one character, it returns that character directly. This is our **Base Case**.

$$\text{reverseString("c")} \rightarrow \text{returns "c"}$$

This is the bottom-most node of our tree.

highlights.text: reverseString("c") returns "c" (base case)

steps.title: Unwind and Return Values
steps.content:
Now we move back up the tree (from the bottom up), substituting the returned values into the waiting expressions:

1.  **From Level 2 to Level 1:** 
    $$\text{reverseString("bc")} = \text{"c"} + \text{'b'} = \text{"cb"}$$

2.  **From Level 1 to Level 0:** 
    $$\text{reverseString("abc")} = \text{"cb"} + \text{'a'} = \text{"cba"}$$

By following the return arrows up the tree, the final result is constructed.

highlights.text: reverseString("bc") returns "c" + 'b' = "cb"

steps.title: Final Result Summary
steps.content:
The execution trace is complete. The total return value for the initial call is:

$$\text{Result} = \text{"cba"}$$

In an IB exam, showing the clear progression from the initial call down to the base case and the subsequent concatenation back up will earn full marks.

steps.title: Understanding the Stack Process
steps.content: To trace a recursive function using a **Stack-Based Trace**, we imagine a physical stack of "frames." Each time the function calls itself, we push a new frame onto the stack. This frame stays there, "waiting," until the call above it returns a value. This concept is a key part of **Topic 5: Abstract Data Structures**.

steps.title: Pushing Calls onto Stack
steps.content:
We start with the initial call and move toward the base case. Each step pushes a new frame onto the stack:

1.  **Call 1:** `reverseString("abc")` is called. It needs to calculate `reverseString("bc") + "a"`. It pauses and waits.
2.  **Call 2:** `reverseString("bc")` is called. It needs to calculate `reverseString("c") + "b"`. It pauses and waits.

$$\begin{bmatrix} \text{reverseString("c")} & \text{(Top)} \\ \text{reverseString("bc")} & \\ \text{reverseString("abc")} & \text{(Bottom)} \end{bmatrix}$$

According to the markscheme, identifying these specific calls is essential for earning marks.

highlights.text: reverseString("abc") calls reverseString("bc") + 'a'
reverseString("bc") calls reverseString("c") + 'b'

steps.title: Reaching the Base Case
steps.content:
The recursion must stop eventually. In this algorithm, when the string length is 1, it simply returns the character itself. This is our **Base Case**.

*   `reverseString("c")` is called. Since it is a single character, it returns `"c"` immediately.

At this point, the stack has reached its maximum height and is ready to start "popping" (removing) frames.

highlights.text: reverseString("c") returns "c" (base case)

steps.title: Popping and Returning Values
steps.content:
Now we move back down the stack, substituting the returned values into the waiting expressions:

1.  **Pop Call 2:** `reverseString("bc")` receives `"c"` from the base case. It calculates `"c" + "b"` and returns **`"cb"`**.
2.  **Pop Call 1:** `reverseString("abc")` receives `"cb"` from the previous step. It calculates `"cb" + "a"` and returns **`"cba"`**.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Step 2 Result: } & "c" + "b" = "cb" \\ \text{Step 1 Result: } & "cb" + "a" = "cba" \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: reverseString("bc") returns "c" + 'b' = "cb"

steps.title: Final Trace Summary
steps.content:
The final execution trace looks like this:

*   `reverseString("abc")` $\rightarrow$ `reverseString("bc") + "a"` 
*   `reverseString("bc")` $\rightarrow$ `reverseString("c") + "b"` 
*   `reverseString("c")` $\rightarrow$ `"c"` (Base Case)
*   `reverseString("bc")` returns `"cb"` 
*   `reverseString("abc")` returns `"cba"` 

This complete logic earns the full 4 marks.

parts.content: Explain why recursion is suitable for this problem.
parts.markscheme:
- Recursion simplifies problems that can be divided into smaller, similar subproblems. <marks>1</marks>
- It provides an elegant solution for reversing strings by processing one character at a time and combining results during the unwind phase. <marks>1</marks>

<note>Maximum 2 marks.</note>

steps.title: Identify identical subproblems
steps.content:
Recursion is ideal for problems that can be broken down into smaller, identical versions of themselves—a core principle of the **Divide and Conquer** approach. 

In the case of a string, it can be viewed as a single character followed by a smaller substring. Reversing the entire string simply requires reversing that smaller substring and then moving the first character to the end. Since the process for reversing the smaller substring is exactly the same as reversing the original string, the problem is inherently recursive.

highlights.text: Recursion simplifies problems that can be divided into smaller, similar subproblems.

steps.title: Analyze the unwinding phase
steps.content:
A recursive approach provides an elegant solution because it handles the logic of "reversing" during the **unwind phase**. 

As the recursion "dives" deeper, it processes one character at a time until it reaches the base case (an empty string or a single character). As the function calls then return (unwind), the results are combined by concatenating the characters in the correct reversed order. This eliminates the need for complex loops or manual pointer management.

highlights.text: It provides an elegant solution for reversing strings by processing one character at a time and combining results during the unwind phase.

steps.title: Relate to IB syllabus
steps.content: This logic is a fundamental part of **Topic 5.1: Abstract Data Structures** and **Topic D.4: Advanced Program Development**. Understanding how to recognize recursive thinking involves identifying when a data structure (like a string) can be treated as a combination of a head element and a tail that follows the same rules.

steps.title: Divide into subproblems
steps.content:
Recursion is suitable because it simplifies problems that can be broken down into smaller, identical subproblems. For a string, reversing it can be viewed as taking the first character, reversing the remaining substring, and then appending the first character to the end. 

This "divide and conquer" approach is a core characteristic of recursive thinking covered in **Topic 5.1: Abstract data structures**.

highlights.text: Recursion simplifies problems that can be divided into smaller, similar subproblems.

steps.title: The Winding Phase
steps.content:
When a recursive function calls itself, the computer uses a **system stack** to keep track of each call. 

During the "winding" phase, we process the string one character at a time. Each character is essentially "stored" in a new layer of the stack while the function waits for the result of the next smaller subproblem. This continues until we reach the base case (an empty string).

steps.title: The Unwinding Phase
steps.content:
The magic of reversal happens during the **unwind phase**. Because a stack is a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** structure, the last character we reached (the end of the string) is the first one processed as the recursion completes. 

As the stack unwinds, the characters are combined in the reverse order of how they were encountered, providing an elegant and efficient solution for string reversal.

highlights.text: It provides an elegant solution for reversing strings by processing one character at a time and combining results during the unwind phase.

questions.specification: A software company is developing a navigation system that uses a recursive algorithm to calculate the shortest path (i.e. the path with the minimum number of edges/steps between nodes in an unweighted graph) between two points.

parts.content: Define the term recursion.
parts.markscheme:
- A programming technique where a method/function calls itself. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It involves breaking down a problem into smaller instances of the same problem. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It must include a base case (termination condition) to stop the recursive calls. <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Any two of the above. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe one application of recursive algorithms in computing, with reference to the navigation system.
parts.markscheme:
- Recursive algorithms are commonly used for traversing hierarchical or networked data structures such as trees or graphs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In a navigation system, locations are modeled as nodes and roads as edges in a graph. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recursion allows the algorithm to explore all branching paths from a starting point to find and compare path lengths (number of edges) to the destination. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Construct a recursive method `calculateShortestPath` that takes a starting point and an endpoint and returns the shortest path as a list of nodes.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct method signature (e.g., `public List<Node> calculateShortestPath(Node start, Node end)`). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Base case that returns a path list when `start` equals `end`. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cycle detection logic (e.g., checking if a node has been visited) to prevent infinite recursion. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recursive call to neighbors of the current node. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Logic that compares multiple possible paths to identify the shortest one (fewest edges/nodes). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correctly constructs the path by adding the current node to the beginning of the shortest sub-path and returning the list. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:

In `removeBook(title)`, remove **only the first** node whose `book` has the given `title`. If no such node exists, the list should remain unchanged.

parts.content: Describe one advantage and one disadvantage of using a linked list for the wishlist.

questions.specification: An email application needs to manage a queue of outgoing messages using a static data structure.

parts.content: Identify two features of the abstract data type (ADT) queue.
parts.markscheme:
- First-In-First-Out (FIFO) ordering <marks>A1</marks>
- Operations include enqueue (add to tail) and dequeue (remove from head) <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify the queue logic
steps.content:
In IB Computer Science, when we talk about an **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** like a queue, we are looking at its logical behavior. The most fundamental characteristic of a queue is its ordering system. Imagine a line of people waiting for coffee: the first person who gets in line is the first one served. 

This behavior is known as **First-In-First-Out (FIFO)**. In our email application, the first email added to the outgoing queue will be the first one sent out.

highlights.text: First-In-First-Out (FIFO)

steps.title: Define standard operations
steps.content:
To manage data within this FIFO structure, a queue uses two specific standard operations:

1.  **Enqueue**: This adds an item to the 'tail' or back of the queue.
2.  **Dequeue**: This removes an item from the 'head' or front of the queue.

Since the question asks for features of the ADT, mentioning these operations is essential as they define how the structure is manipulated.

highlights.text: enqueue
dequeue

steps.title: Final Review of Features
steps.content:
To secure both marks, we should clearly state the two distinct features identified through the standard characteristics of a queue:

*   **Feature 1**: It follows a **First-In-First-Out (FIFO)** logic.
*   **Feature 2**: It utilizes **Enqueue and Dequeue** operations to add and remove data.

This directly matches the requirements for Topic 5.1.5 (Queues) in your syllabus.

parts.content: Describe one application of a queue in computing, with reference to the email application.
parts.markscheme:
- Queues are used to manage tasks or data packets waiting for processing in the order they were received <marks>A1</marks>
- In the email application, outgoing messages are queued to be sent sequentially to ensure they are dispatched in the correct submission order <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Define FIFO Logic
steps.content:
To understand how a queue works, we first look at its core logic: **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)**. In computing, a queue is an abstract data structure where the first element added is the first one to be removed. 

This ensures that data is processed fairly and in the exact sequence it arrived. If you're studying **Topic 5: Abstract data structures**, you'll recognize this as the fundamental characteristic of a queue.

steps.title: General Task Management
steps.content:
In a broader computing context, queues are used to manage tasks or data packets waiting for processing. When multiple requests arrive at a CPU or a printer at once, they can't all be handled simultaneously. 

The system places them in a queue so they can be handled in the **order they were received**. This prevents data loss and ensures every task eventually gets its turn.

highlights.text: Queues are used to manage tasks or data packets waiting for processing in the order they were received

steps.title: Application to Email
steps.content:
Applying this to the email application, the queue manages the flow of outgoing messages. When you hit 'send' on several emails quickly, the application places them into the queue structure.

Because of the FIFO logic, the messages are sent **sequentially**. This ensures that the first email you finished writing is the first one dispatched to the server, maintaining the correct **submission order**.

highlights.text: outgoing messages are queued to be sent sequentially to ensure they are dispatched in the correct submission order
manage a queue of outgoing messages using a static data structure
